perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of each Deliverable, On2 shall have a period of thirty (30) working days within which to inspect such Deliverable based upon the relevant Specification (an "Acceptance Period"). Should On2 find that any Deliverable does not substantially conform to the relevant Specification, On2 shall promptly so notify Wildform in writing, which notice shall provide sufficiently detailed explanation of the non-conformities so as to allow Wildform to reproduce them (any such notice is referred to herein as a "Nonconformance Notice"). Each of the Deliverables shall be deemed accepted by On2 unless On2 delivers to Wildform a Nonconformance Notice within thirty (30) business days of the delivery of such Deliverable.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of each Deliverable, On2 shall have a period of ten (10) working days within which to inspect such Deliverable based upon the relevant Specification (an "Acceptance Period"). Should On2 find that any Deliverable does not substantially conform to the relevant Specification, On2 shall promptly so notify Wildform in writing, which notice shall provide sufficiently detailed explanation of the non-conformities so as to allow Wildform to reproduce them (any such notice is referred to herein as a "Nonconformance Notice"). Each of the Deliverables shall be deemed accepted by On2 unless On2 delivers to Wildform a Nonconformance Notice within ten (10) business days of the delivery of such Deliverable.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the acceptance period from 30 days to 10 days could be problematic under certain interpretations of commercial law, potentially creating unreasonable constraints for inspection and acceptance of deliverables.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-602. Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-602 states that rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. Reducing the inspection period to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity of the Deliverables, effectively hindering On2's ability to rightfully reject non-conforming goods, thus potentially conflicting with the UCC's provision for a 'reasonable time'.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the acceptance period from 30 to 10 days, which the Law Explanation, based on UCC § 2-602, suggests may be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity of the Deliverables. This hinders On2's ability to reject non-conforming goods, potentially conflicting with the UCC's provision for a 'reasonable time'. Given the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, reducing the time frame significantly raises concerns about conflicting with legal standards of 'reasonableness' in commercial transactions, which the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition explicitly highlights.

perturbation.original_text: During the first twelve (12) months of the Term, Wildform will provide On2 with reasonable technical support for the Deliverables via phone and email during Wildform's normal business hours with respect to the following: o Train On2 personnel in the way the code works. o Train On2 personnel in how the software is designed, maintained and compiled. o Provide ongoing support during the 12-month term for On2's further development and integration.
perturbation.changed_text: During the first one (1) month of the Term, Wildform will provide On2 with reasonable technical support for the Deliverables via phone and email during Wildform's normal business hours with respect to the following: o Train On2 personnel in the way the code works. o Train On2 personnel in how the software is designed, maintained and compiled. o Provide ongoing support during the 1-month term for On2's further development and integration.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the support period from 12 months to 1 month creates uncertainty regarding the adequacy of support, potentially conflicting with implied warranty principles, especially if the software is complex or has latent defects. Depending on the context, the limited support period could be viewed as unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: By drastically reducing the technical support period, the contract may be seen as undermining the implied warranty of merchantability. While disclaimers are generally permissible, a one-month support window for complex software could be deemed insufficient to ensure the goods are fit for the ordinary purpose for which they are used, potentially conflicting with UCC 2-314's expectations for merchantability.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract drastically reduces the technical support period from 12 months to 1 month. The Law Explanation says this undermines the implied warranty of merchantability, potentially conflicting with UCC 2-314's expectations for merchantability. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by saying that depending on the context, the limited support period could be viewed as unconscionable. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', where a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: Failure by Wildform to provide a correction for a Program Error within the specified period shall not be deemed a breach of this Agreement provided that Wildform has used commercially reasonable efforts to correct such Program Error.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure by Wildform to provide a correction for a Program Error within the specified period shall not be deemed a breach of this Agreement, regardless of whether Wildform has used commercially reasonable efforts to correct such Program Error.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'commercially reasonable efforts' clause effectively eliminates any obligation for Wildform to diligently address Program Errors, contradicting standard contractual duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Removing the requirement for 'commercially reasonable efforts' allows Wildform to completely disregard Program Errors without consequence. This effectively allows bad-faith performance and undermines the reasonable expectations of On2, thus contradicting the UCC.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for Wildform to use "commercially reasonable efforts" to correct program errors. The Law Explanation states this contradicts UCC § 1-304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in contract performance and enforcement. By removing the 'commercially reasonable efforts' clause, Wildform can disregard program errors without consequence, undermining the reasonable expectations of On2. Although the scraped law snippets don't directly quote UCC § 1-304, the Law Explanation explicitly makes this connection, and the Perturbation Explanation supports the claim that this change eliminates an obligation, leading to a contradiction.
